corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: We propose a new test to measure a text model's multitask accuracy.
We propose a new test to measure a text model's multitask accuracy.
To attain high accuracy on this test, models must possess extensive world knowledge and problem solving ability.
We find that while most recent models have near random-chance accuracy, the very largest GPT-3 model improves over random chance by almost 20 percentage points on average.
We find that while most recent models have near random-chance accuracy, the very largest GPT-3 model improves over random chance by almost 20 percentage points on average.
However, on every one of the 57 tasks, the best models still need substantial improvements before they can reach expert-level accuracy.
Worse, they still have nearrandom accuracy on some socially important subjects such as morality and law.

The General Language Understanding Evaluation benchmark (GLUE) was introduced in 2018 to evaluate performance on a wide range of NLP tasks, and top models achieved superhuman performance within a year.
To address the shortcomings of GLUE, researchers designed the SuperGLUE benchmark with more difficult tasks .

To bridge the gap between the wide-ranging knowledge that models see during pretraining and the existing measures of success, we introduce a new benchmark for assessing models across a diverse set of subjects that humans learn.

To bridge the gap between the wide-ranging knowledge that models see during pretraining and the existing measures of success, we introduce a new benchmark for assessing models across a diverse set of subjects that humans learn.
We design the benchmark to measure knowledge acquired during pretraining by evaluating models exclusively in zero-shot and few-shot settings.
We design the benchmark to measure knowledge acquired during pretraining by evaluating models exclusively in zero-shot and few-shot settings.
This makes the benchmark more challenging and more similar to how we evaluate humans.
The benchmark covers 57 subjects across STEM, the humanities, the social sciences, and more.

With the advent of strong zero-shot and few-shot learning, it is now possible to curate a diverse set of tasks for evaluation and remove the possibility of models on "spurious cues" in a dataset to achieve high performance.
However, NLG is notoriously difficult to evaluate and lacks a standard metric .
Consequently, we instead create a simple-to-evaluate test that measures classification accuracy on multiple choice questions.
We compare the few-shot accuracy of each GPT-3 size in Table .
We find that the three smaller GPT-3 models have near random accuracy (around 25%).
In contrast, we find that the X-Large 175 billion parameter GPT-3 model performs substantially better than random, with an accuracy of 43.9%.

While the smaller models have around 25% zero-shot accuracy, Figure in Appendix A shows that the largest GPT-3 model has a much higher zero-shot accuracy of about 37.7%. also observe that larger GPT-3 models perform better, though progress tends to be steadier.
In Figure we show that non-random accuracy on the multitask test emerged with recent large few-shot models compared to datasets that assess commonsense and linguistic understanding.
We also find that even the smallest UnifiedQA variant, with just 60 million parameters, has approximately 29.3% accuracy.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
